title: Mural and Commissioned Art Business
metaTitle: How to Start a Mural Art Business: Real Costs, Earnings, and Steps
metaDescription: Honest breakdown of starting a mural and commissioned art business: real startup costs, realistic per-project earnings, finding clients, and what most muralists get wrong.
bestFor: Skilled painters who can scale up artwork, manage physical job sites, and sell ideas through proposals and renderings
biggestRisk: Long gaps between large projects combined with underbidding the real time, prep, and equipment a wall actually takes
whatItIs: A mural and commissioned art business paints large-scale artwork for businesses, homeowners, schools, and cities, interior and exterior walls, restaurant feature walls, retail backdrops, office branding, community and public art, alongside smaller commissioned pieces like custom canvases and signage. You are paid per project or per square foot, and the work blends real artistic skill with project management: measuring walls, preparing surfaces, sourcing the right paints, working at height on ladders or lifts, and managing weather and timelines. Unlike fine artists who sell finished work, muralists sell a concept first, usually through a proposal and a mockup or rendering, then execute on the client's site.
dailyReality: Project days are physical and long, priming and prepping the surface, projecting or gridding your design onto the wall, then painting for many hours, often on a ladder, scaffold, or scissor lift, in sun, wind, or a noisy occupied business. Between projects, much of your week is unpaid business work: site visits to measure and quote, creating digital mockups and proposals, sourcing exterior-grade paints and anti-graffiti coatings, and chasing leads and deposits. A single mural can run from two days to several weeks, so your calendar swings between intense on-site stretches and slower stretches of proposals and networking.

startupCostsItemized.item: Quality acrylics, exterior-grade paints, primers, and brushes/rollers
Ladders, drop cloths, painter's tape, basic site gear
Projector or grid tools for transferring designs
Spray equipment and respirator (for aerosol/large work)
Liability insurance (often required for commercial and public jobs)
Portfolio website and photography of work
Scissor lift or scaffold rental (per large exterior job)

realEarnings.yearOne: Beginners often earn very little in slow months and $0 to $3,000 in busier ones during year one, landing a handful of smaller interior murals and commissioned pieces. Early pricing commonly runs $10 to $30 per square foot or a few hundred to a few thousand dollars per project while building a portfolio.
realEarnings.experienced: Established muralists with a strong portfolio commonly report $3,000 to $8,000 per month averaged across the year, with project rates of $25 to $60 per square foot and commercial murals frequently landing in the $3,000 to $15,000 range depending on size, detail, and height.
realEarnings.topEarners: In-demand muralists and small studios reach $10,000 to $25,000 per month in busy seasons through large commercial commissions, public art grants, multi-wall corporate branding contracts, and licensing their imagery. Reaching this takes years of recognizable style, public installations, relationships with developers, designers, and city arts programs, and the ability to manage crews and large lifts.
realEarnings.whatAffectsMost: The single biggest factor is client type and scale: commercial, civic, and developer projects pay multiples of residential ones. After that, accurate bidding of prep, height, and time, and a distinctive style that makes you the obvious choice, matter far more than how cheap your paint is.
realEarnings.perHour: Effective rates vary wildly with bidding skill. Well-priced commercial muralists clear $40 to $100 per hour of actual painting, but unpaid proposal, travel, and prep time pulls realistic blended rates down to $25 to $70, and underbid jobs can drop below minimum wage.

howToStart: Month 1: Develop a small body of mural-style work, paint a wall in your home, a friend's business, or a community space at low or no cost, and photograph it well. A muralist with no large-scale photos cannot win paid work, so building visible proof comes first.

Month 2: Learn to produce professional proposals with a digital mockup of your art on a photo of the client's actual wall, plus clear pricing (per square foot or per project), a deposit policy, and a timeline. Get liability insurance, which most commercial and city jobs require.

Days 30-90: Approach local cafes, gyms, breweries, boutiques, schools, and property developers with your portfolio and a proposal for a specific wall. Apply to city and nonprofit public art calls, which are a major source of larger, well-paid commissions.

Months 3-6: Take a deposit on every job, document each finished mural for your portfolio and social media, and build relationships with interior designers, sign companies, and developers who can feed you repeat work.

mustHave: Genuine drawing and painting skill, plus the ability to scale a small design accurately onto a large wall
Comfort and safety working at height on ladders, scaffolds, and lifts for long stretches
Project discipline, measuring, prepping surfaces correctly, and finishing on a committed timeline
canLearn: Surface preparation, exterior-grade paints, and protective coatings for outdoor durability
Creating digital mockups and professional proposals to sell a concept before painting
Pricing by square foot and project while accounting for prep, height, and travel
separators: A recognizable personal style that makes clients seek you specifically rather than the cheapest bidder
Winning commercial, developer, and public/civic commissions instead of low-paying residential work
Accurate bidding and surface knowledge so jobs finish on time, on budget, and without callbacks for peeling or fading

whatPeopleGetWrong: Underbidding by pricing only the painting and ignoring prep, priming, travel, lift rental, and the many unpaid hours of proposals
Using interior or hobby-grade paint outdoors, then watching murals fade or peel within a year and damaging their reputation
Skipping surface preparation, so even great artwork fails because the wall was never properly cleaned, primed, or sealed
Treating it as pure art and neglecting contracts, deposits, and timelines, leading to scope creep and unpaid work
Ignoring safety at height, falls and improper lift use are real risks that insurance and training exist to manage
Painting only residential murals at low rates instead of pursuing the commercial, civic, and developer work that actually pays

toolsAndEquipment.item: Professional brushes, rollers, and exterior-grade paints
toolsAndEquipment.note: Outdoor murals demand UV- and weather-resistant paint; cheap paint fails fast and costs you the reputation.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Ladders, drop cloths, tape, and site gear
toolsAndEquipment.note: Basic but essential. Add a respirator if you spray.
How you get an accurate design onto a large wall quickly.
For large-scale and aerosol work; learn proper technique and safety first.
Protects exterior murals and is often expected by commercial clients.

toolsAndEquipment.item: Scissor lift or scaffold (rented per job)
toolsAndEquipment.note: Needed for tall exterior walls; build the rental cost into the bid, do not buy early.

methods: Direct proposals to local businesses with blank walls, cafes, breweries, gyms, boutiques, offices, using a mockup of your art on their wall
Applying to city, county, and nonprofit public art calls and grants, a major source of larger commissions
An image-led portfolio on Instagram and a website, since mural work sells visually
Relationships with interior designers, architects, sign companies, and property developers who recommend muralists
Documented, photographed installations that double as advertising in high-traffic locations
howToFindCustomers.whereCustomersAre: Small businesses wanting a feature or photo-worthy wall, developers and property managers branding spaces, schools and nonprofits, city arts programs, and homeowners wanting custom interiors. The best-paying clients reach you through public art calls, designers, and developer networks rather than consumer ads.
howToFindCustomers.timeToClientBase: First commissions often come within one to three months of having a real portfolio, but a steady flow of well-paid projects typically takes one to two years of visible installations and relationships. Mural work is lumpy, one large project can carry several months.
howToFindCustomers.wasteOfTime: Paid social ads and generic flyers early on. Muralists are hired off visible work and word of mouth; one striking, well-located mural with a photo credit drives more leads than any ad.

howItScales.toFullTime: Yes, but only by moving up to commercial, civic, and developer commissions and pricing accurately. Residential murals alone rarely support full-time income; a calendar of larger contracts and public art does.
howItScales.hireAndStepBack: Partially. Established muralists hire assistants to prep, base-coat, and fill while reserving the signature work for themselves, which raises throughput. Fully stepping back is hard because clients buy your specific style and name.
howItScales.sellable: A personal-brand mural practice is difficult to sell. A studio with recurring commercial contracts, a team, a documented process, and licensable imagery or a brand independent of one artist has more resale potential, though it remains modest.
howItScales.whatScalingRequires: Reliable assistants, standardized prep and quality processes, equipment for working at height safely, a steady pipeline of commercial and civic clients, and possibly licensing your designs for additional revenue.

strongFit: You have real painting skill and can scale designs accurately onto large surfaces
You are physically able and comfortable working at height for long hours
You can sell a concept through proposals and mockups, not just finished art
You can handle irregular income and lumpy project timelines
poorFit: You want steady, predictable monthly income
You are uncomfortable on ladders, lifts, or scaffolding
You dislike the business side, bidding, contracts, deposits, and client management
You are unwilling to do unglamorous prep work and outdoor labor in the elements
askYourself: Can I price a job to cover prep, height, travel, and proposal time and still finish profitably?
Do I have a distinctive enough style to be chosen over the cheapest bidder?
Is there enough commercial, civic, and developer demand in my area to fill the gaps between big projects?

faqs.question: Do I need a permit to paint a mural?
faqs.answer: Often, yes, especially for exterior and public-facing walls. Many cities require permits, design review, or property-owner authorization, and some have specific mural or sign ordinances. Always confirm permitting and get written owner permission before painting; this is part of why working with city arts programs, which handle approvals, is attractive.

faqs.question: How do I price a mural?
faqs.answer: Most muralists price per square foot or per project, with rates rising for detail, height, exterior durability requirements, and tight timelines. Beginners often charge $10 to $30 per square foot and experienced artists $25 to $60 or more. Whatever method you use, build in surface prep, travel, proposal time, and any lift rental, since these quietly eat profit.

faqs.question: What paint should I use for an outdoor mural?
faqs.answer: Use exterior-grade, UV-resistant acrylics or specialized mural paints over a properly cleaned and primed surface, then finish with a protective or anti-graffiti topcoat. Interior or hobby paints fade and peel outdoors within a season or two, which leads to callbacks and reputation damage.

faqs.question: How do I find my first mural clients?
faqs.answer: Build a real portfolio first, even from low-paid or volunteer walls, then approach local businesses with blank, visible walls using a mockup of your art on their actual space. Public art calls from cities and nonprofits are another strong source of paid first projects. Designers and developers become repeat referrers once you have visible work.

faqs.question: Is liability insurance necessary?
faqs.answer: For most commercial, civic, and public projects, yes, clients and cities frequently require proof of general liability insurance, partly because you are working at height on their property. It is affordable annually and protects you against the real risks of ladders, lifts, and paint damage.

faqs.question: Can I do this part-time around a job?
faqs.answer: It is difficult. Murals require multi-day on-site stretches in daylight, often on weekdays for commercial clients, plus site visits and proposals. Small commissioned pieces and the occasional weekend interior mural can be done part-time, but the well-paid commercial and exterior work is hard to fit around a strict full-time schedule.

faqs.question: How is this different from being a fine artist?
faqs.answer: Fine artists create and sell finished work, often speculatively. Muralists sell a concept first, through a proposal and mockup, then execute it on the client's site under real-world constraints of scale, weather, height, and deadlines. It is as much project management and client work as it is art.
